You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```

實際的補救就是讓這把 token 失效,兩分鐘可以做完:

1. 在 Telegram 找 **@BotFather** → `/revoke` → 選 `@JiunchiAI02Bot`,它會立刻作廢舊 token 並給你一把新的。
2. 把新 token 更新到 bridge 的 `.env`(`BOT_TOKEN`),重啟 bridge。

換掉之後,留在這份對話記錄裡的舊 token 就只是一串無效字串,風險歸零。

順帶說明實際風險等級讓你判斷急迫性:這份記錄存在你本機的 Claude Code session 檔案裡,不是公開的;但 token 本身等於 bot 的完全控制權(能收發訊息、讀 bot 收到的內容),而且它也可能存在其他地方的 log(例如 bridge 自己的 console log 若有落檔)。所以不算緊急事故,但值得順手換掉。

另外一個之後可以做的小改進:grammY 的錯誤物件會把 `api.token` 一起 dump 出來(你貼的 log 就是這樣洩的),bridge 的 error handler 可以在印 log 前把 token 遮罩掉。要的話我可以順手加。

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The user requested "順手加 token 遮罩,然後一起 commit" (casually add token masking, then commit together).

From Claude's response, I can see:
1. There was a discussion about a token that was accidentally exposed in conversation logs
2. Claude explained that the conversation logs are immutable and can't be edited
3. Claude recommended revoking the token via @BotFather and getting a new one
4. Claude explained the actual risk level - the token exists in local Claude Code session files, not public, but gives full bot control
5. Claude mentioned that grammY's error objects dump `api.token` together (which is how the token was leaked in the log the user posted)
6. Claude offered to add error handler improvements to mask the token in logs before printing

Now there's a grep operation looking for `bot\.catch|Bot error` in the src directory, and it found the error handler at lines 1375-1377 in src/index.ts:

```typescript
bot.catch((err) => {
  console.error("Bot error:", err);
});
```

This is the error handler that needs to be modified to mask the token before logging.

So the work being done is:
1. Locating the error handler that logs bot errors (just completed via grep)
2. Planning to modify it to mask tokens before logging
3. Then commit the changes

This is a security improvement to prevent token leakage in error logs.

content.thinking:
The user is editing the src/index.ts file to add an import for `inspect` from `node:util`. This is the first step in implementing token masking functionality. The import statement `import { inspect } from "node:util";` was added after the pipeline import and before the path import.

This is part of the work to add token masking to error logs to prevent token leakage when grammY error objects are logged.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Added node:util inspect import for error sanitization</title>
  <subtitle>Imported inspect utility to prepare for token masking in error logs</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Added import statement `import { inspect } from "node:util";` to src/index.ts line 6</fact>
    <fact>The inspect utility will be used to safely serialize error objects while masking sensitive tokens</fact>
    <fact>This addresses the security issue where grammY error objects expose api.token in their dump output</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Added the inspect import from Node.js's util module to src/index.ts as preparation for implementing token masking in the bot error handler. The inspect utility will be used to safely serialize error objects and redact sensitive information (specifically the bot token) before logging. This is part of addressing a real security incident where a token was exposed in error logs because grammY's error objects include the api.token field in their output.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/index.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

The user has implemented token masking in the bot error handler. Let me analyze what was done:

1. The bot.catch() error handler was modified
2. Instead of directly logging the error object (which would expose the token), it now:
   - Uses `inspect(err, { depth: 4 })` to serialize the error to a string
   - Uses `.replaceAll(config.telegramToken, "***TOKEN***")` to mask the token
   - Logs the sanitized string instead

This is a security improvement that addresses the token leak issue discovered earlier when grammY error objects dump the api.token in their output.

The Chinese comments explain:
- "grammY 的 BotError 帶整個 ctx，直接 console.error 會把 ctx.api.token"
- "dump 進 log；先字串化再遮罩 token。"
Which translates to: "grammY's BotError carries the entire ctx, directly console.error would dump ctx.api.token into the log; stringify first then mask the token."

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Implemented token masking in bot error handler</title>
  <subtitle>Bot errors now sanitize and mask telegram tokens before logging to prevent credential exposure</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Modified bot.catch() error handler in src/index.ts at line 1375-1377 to sanitize error objects before logging</fact>
    <fact>Error objects are serialized using inspect(err, {depth: 4}) then tokens are masked with replaceAll(config.telegramToken, "***TOKEN***")</fact>
    <fact>This prevents grammY's BotError objects from exposing ctx.api.token in console logs</fact>
    <fact>The implementation uses Node.js util.inspect for safe serialization and string replacement for masking</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented security-critical token masking in the Telegram bot's global error handler. When grammY encounters errors, its BotError objects contain the full context including ctx.api.token, which would be exposed in logs with direct console.error calls. The new implementation serializes the error object to a string using Node's inspect utility (depth limited to 4 levels for performance), then uses replaceAll to mask any occurrences of the bot token with "***TOKEN***" before logging. This closes a security gap that caused a real token leak incident earlier in the session, following the same pattern already used elsewhere in the codebase for environment variable sanitization when spawning subprocesses.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Committed token masking security fix and status dashboard link restoration</title>
  <subtitle>Created commit 04be380 with bot error handler token masking and status command URL fix</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Committed changes to src/index.ts and src/commands/status.ts as commit 04be380</fact>
    <fact>The /status command now includes the dashboard URL at the end of its response (uses 127.0.0.1 instead of localhost for Telegram link compatibility)</fact>
    <fact>The bot.catch() error handler now uses util.inspect to serialize errors and replaceAll to mask config.telegramToken before logging</fact>
    <fact>This prevents grammY BotError objects from exposing ctx.api.token in console logs, closing a real security vulnerability</fact>
    <fact>Commit message documents that the status dashboard URL was accidentally removed in commit 7d10380 when Mini App code was extracted</fact>
    <fact>STATUS_HTTPS_URL environment variable takes precedence when set for the dashboard link</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Successfully committed two related improvements in commit 04be380: restored the dashboard URL link in the /status command response (which was inadvertently removed during Mini App refactoring) and implemented critical token masking in the bot's global error handler. The status fix ensures users can still access the monitoring dashboard at 127.0.0.1 (IP format used because Telegram rejects localhost URLs), with HTTPS override support via STATUS_HTTPS_URL. The security fix addresses a real incident where a bot token was exposed in error logs by serializing error objects with util.inspect and masking tokens with replaceAll before logging.
